questions.text: Why can't a smart contract directly call an external web API?

options.text: Because external API responses are non-deterministic, and different nodes might get different results, breaking consensus.
Because Solidity doesn't support HTTP requests.
Because it would cost too much gas.
Because APIs are too slow for blockchain.

questions.explanation: Blockchain consensus requires that every node can independently verify the same result. External API calls would return different results at different times or for different nodes.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Blockchain consensus requires that every node can independently verify the same result. External API calls would return different results at different times or for different nodes.

questions.text: What is the primary risk of using a centralised oracle?

options.text: Single point of failure - if the oracle operator is compromised or goes offline, the dApp is affected.
It uses too much gas.
It only works with Ethereum.
It requires Chainlink tokens.

questions.explanation: A centralised oracle is controlled by one entity, creating a single point of trust and failure that contradicts the decentralised ethos of blockchain.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

A centralised oracle is controlled by one entity, creating a single point of trust and failure that contradicts the decentralised ethos of blockchain.

questions.text: How does Chainlink achieve decentralisation in its oracle network?

options.text: By using multiple independent node operators who fetch data from multiple sources, with economic incentives for accurate reporting.
By running all nodes on a single server.
By using a single API for all data.
By requiring users to run their own nodes.

questions.explanation: Chainlink's decentralised oracle network uses many independent nodes with staked LINK tokens to provide data, and aggregates their responses to avoid reliance on any single node.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Chainlink's decentralised oracle network uses many independent nodes with staked LINK tokens to provide data, and aggregates their responses to avoid reliance on any single node.

questions.text: What role do LINK tokens play in the Chainlink network?

options.text: They are staked by node operators as collateral to incentivise honest data reporting.
They are used to pay gas fees on Ethereum.
They are the only currency accepted for oracle requests.
They are burned when data is submitted.

questions.explanation: LINK is staked by oracle nodes; if they submit bad data, their stake can be slashed. This economic model secures the network.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

LINK is staked by oracle nodes; if they submit bad data, their stake can be slashed. This economic model secures the network.

questions.text: How does MST's WASMify fundamentally differ from Chainlink as an oracle solution?

options.text: WASMify uses zero-knowledge proofs to verify off-chain computation, eliminating the need to trust any node operator.
WASMify is a centralised oracle run by MST.
WASMify only works for price feeds.
WASMify requires a network of staked validators like Chainlink.

questions.explanation: WASMify shifts trust from economic incentives (staking) to cryptographic verification (ZK proofs), allowing trustless oracle operation without a network of nodes.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

WASMify shifts trust from economic incentives (staking) to cryptographic verification (ZK proofs), allowing trustless oracle operation without a network of nodes.

questions.text: What is a key advantage of using WASMify for IoT or complex data oracles?

options.text: It can process large, complex data off-chain and only submit a small ZK proof on-chain, saving gas and maintaining privacy.
It automatically connects to all IoT devices.
It requires no programming.
It is free to use.

questions.explanation: WASMify enables heavy computation off-chain, reducing on-chain data requirements to a tiny proof - ideal for IoT streams, ML models, and private data.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

WASMify enables heavy computation off-chain, reducing on-chain data requirements to a tiny proof - ideal for IoT streams, ML models, and private data.

questions.text: In the Chainlink aggregation process, what happens if one node submits a price far different from others?

options.text: The aggregation contract calculates the median, so outliers have minimal impact, and the outlier node may be penalised.
The whole feed shuts down.
That node's data is automatically accepted as correct.
The node receives a bonus for being different.

questions.explanation: Median aggregation makes the feed resistant to outliers. Nodes submitting extreme values risk losing their staked LINK.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Median aggregation makes the feed resistant to outliers. Nodes submitting extreme values risk losing their staked LINK.

questions.text: What does "deterministic" mean in the context of blockchain execution?

options.text: Given the same inputs and state, every node must produce exactly the same output.
The transaction order is random.
Only one node executes the transaction.
The result depends on the time of day.

questions.explanation: Determinism is essential for consensus: all nodes must agree on the new state after a transaction.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Determinism is essential for consensus: all nodes must agree on the new state after a transaction.

questions.text: Which of the following is a realistic use case for a ZK-powered oracle like WASMify?

options.text: Verifying that a cargo container maintained a specific temperature range using IoT sensor data, without revealing the full data log on-chain.
Checking the current time.
Validating a simple ERC-20 transfer.
Generating random numbers.

questions.explanation: ZK oracles excel at complex, private data verification - processing large sensor logs and proving compliance without exposing sensitive data.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

ZK oracles excel at complex, private data verification - processing large sensor logs and proving compliance without exposing sensitive data.

questions.text: Why is the Oracle Problem considered one of the hardest challenges in blockchain development?

options.text: Because it requires bridging the trustless, deterministic on-chain world with the non-deterministic, trust-based off-chain world without reintroducing centralisation.
Because Solidity doesn't have oracle support.
Because oracles are expensive to deploy.
Because there is no solution yet.

questions.explanation: The Oracle Problem is fundamentally about maintaining blockchain's trustless properties while still gaining access to real-world data - a delicate balance.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

The Oracle Problem is fundamentally about maintaining blockchain's trustless properties while still gaining access to real-world data - a delicate balance.
